pi_items.source: Task files (task.json, setup_task.sh, export_result.sh) specify this output path.
Task files (task.json, setup_task.sh) and the environment's install_gvsig.sh script confirm this download path.
Mathematical calculation: a 20x20 degree global grid (-180 to 180, -90 to 90) requires 360/20 * 180/20 = 18 * 9 = 162 polygon cells. The lower bound of 150 is valid.
Mathematical calculation: a 20x20 degree global grid requires exactly 162 polygon cells. The upper bound of 180 is valid.
Downloaded 'ne_110m_populated_places.shp' from the Natural Earth URL (naciscdn.org) used in install_gvsig.sh, then ran a Python script (Geopandas) confirming it contains exactly 243 point features.
Verified as the task's stated tolerance threshold to accommodate slight variations in how spatial joins are handled near grid boundaries.

privileged_info_summary: The input dataset is the real Natural Earth 'ne_110m_populated_places.shp' file which is confirmed to contain exactly 243 point features. The expected task output is a 20x20 degree global grid (from -180 to 180 longitude, -90 to 90 latitude), which mathematically necessitates the creation of exactly 162 polygon cells (18x9). The expected feature count bounds (150-180) correctly bracket this required cell count. Since all 243 cities fall well within the standard global extent (no points lie directly on the 20-degree grid boundaries), a point-in-polygon spatial join will result in a total count of exactly 243 cities appropriately distributed across the grid cells.
